citation: LaPlaca DA, McCullick BA. National Football League Scouting Combine Test Results of Elite-Level National Football League Players. Research Quarterly for Exercise and Sport. Published online February 7, 2025. doi:10.1080/02701367.2024.2448827
abstract: This secondary analysis used NFL Scouting Combine and early-career NFL participation data to identify position-specific combine test results associated with elite-level NFL game participation. The authors analyzed all offensive and defensive players who attended the combine from 2006-2016 (N=3,438) and grouped athletes by whether they averaged at least 10 NFL games played per active season from 2006-2018. Combine measures included 40-yard dash, vertical jump, bench press, broad jump, three-cone drill, shuttle run, height, and weight across 15 position groups. Every position group had at least one combine measure that significantly differentiated players averaging at least 10 games per season from those below that threshold. The study provides normative and position-specific benchmarks intended to help teams identify the physical tests most relevant to durable early-career NFL participation.
tldr: Combine testing is not one-size-fits-all. Across 15 NFL position groups, at least one physical test separated players who became regular game participants from those who did not. For Baylor football staff, the main value is not treating the 40-yard dash or any single drill as universally predictive, but building position-specific profiles where the most relevant movement qualities differ by role.
methods: Secondary data analysis of publicly available Pro Football Reference combine records. The sample included offensive and defensive players attending the NFL Scouting Combine from 2006-2016 across QB, FB, RB, WR, TE, OT, G, C, CB, FS, SS, OLB, ILB, DE, and DT. NFL participation was tracked from 2006-2018, allowing at least three professional seasons after draft eligibility. Players were grouped by average active-roster games played per season: at least 10 games versus fewer than 10 games. Descriptive statistics and independent-samples t-tests compared combine measures by position group.
findings: For each of the 15 position groups, at least one combine metric significantly differed between players who averaged at least 10 games per season and those who did not. The relevant metric varied by position, reinforcing that physical testing must be interpreted through role demands. The paper reports position-specific averages and ranges for successful versus less successful early-career players, giving scouts and performance staff concrete benchmark values rather than only correlations.
limitations: The study uses retrospective public data and games played as the main success marker, which does not separate injury, roster context, coaching decisions, scheme fit, draft capital, or true performance quality. Combine participation and missing tests may bias results. The analysis is observational, so test results should be treated as screening information rather than causal determinants of NFL success. The 2006-2016 cohort may not perfectly reflect current player development, tactics, or testing preparation.
practicalImplications: Use combine-style testing as a position-specific profiling tool, not a universal ranking system. For Baylor football, benchmarks can help identify whether an athlete has physical traits aligned with his projected role, but decisions should combine testing with film, practice load tolerance, medical history, and position skill. Staff can also use these norms to set development targets for athletes seeking professional evaluation.
athleteDev: Long-term development plans should map physical qualities to role demands. Skill players, linemen, linebackers, and specialists need different combinations of acceleration, power, agility, size, and strength endurance. The paper supports building position-specific testing dashboards and avoiding overemphasis on a single marquee combine number when guiding athletes toward NFL readiness.
rtp: The paper is not an injury return-to-play study, but its position-specific benchmarks can provide late-stage performance targets after injury. A returning athlete should be compared with relevant positional norms and his own baseline, especially for the qualities most tied to participation at his position, before unrestricted football exposure.

citation: Vincent LM, Blissmer BJ, Hatfield DL. National Scouting Combine scores as performance predictors in the National Football League. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2018;XX(X):000-000.
abstract: This study examined whether National Scouting Combine measures predict early NFL on-field performance by position. Combine participants from 2005-2010 who subsequently played in the NFL were analyzed at quarterback (n=44), running back (n=82), wide receiver (n=116), linebacker (n=139), defensive end (n=59), and defensive tackle (n=72). Raw combine scores included 40-yard dash, countermovement vertical jump, standing long jump, and 20-yard shuttle; calculated horizontal and vertical power variables were also tested. At least one combine measure correlated with on-field performance at every position, but predictive strength varied widely. Regression models explained 4%-62% of selected performance outcomes, with stronger relationships for quarterback rushing, running back production, and defensive end tackling than for wide receiver and defensive tackle outcomes. The authors recommend adding power calculations and more position-specific tests to the combine battery.
tldr: Combine tests can predict some NFL outcomes, but only modestly and differently by position. Speed and broad jump mattered for running backs, power measures were useful for quarterbacks and defensive ends, and the standard shuttle had limited broad value. For Baylor staff, the lesson is to convert raw testing into role-specific performance questions rather than assume a better test number automatically means better football output.
methods: Retrospective observational study of NFL combine attendees from 2005-2010 who recorded position-specific NFL statistics over their first four seasons for quarterbacks and first three seasons for all other positions. Positions analyzed were QB, RB, WR, LB, DE, and DT. Combine variables were 40-yard dash time, countermovement vertical jump, standing long jump, 20-yard shuttle, and calculated horizontal/vertical power measures. Pearson correlations assessed associations with on-field statistics, and hierarchical linear regression tested how much variance combine variables explained within each position.
findings: At quarterback, power variables helped explain rushing attempts and rushing yards, with grouped power and non-power variables accounting for up to 62.2% of variance in rushing yards. At running back, 40-yard dash and standing long jump predicted rushing yards, attempts, and yards per game, while horizontal power plus 40-yard time related to touchdowns. Wide receiver relationships were minimal. At linebacker, sacks related to multiple combine measures and total variance explained was modest. At defensive end, horizontal and vertical power predicted tackling outcomes and vertical jump/standing long jump related to sacks. Defensive tackle prediction was limited, with standing long jump explaining a small portion of sack variance.
limitations: The study is retrospective and limited to players who both attended the combine and later accumulated NFL statistics, which may exclude important selection effects. Performance statistics are position-dependent and influenced by scheme, opportunity, health, depth chart, and teammate quality. Some combine events had missing data by position. The analysis covers a 2005-2010 cohort and may not reflect current training preparation, tactical trends, or athlete profiles.
practicalImplications: Testing batteries should include power interpretation, not just raw times and jump heights. For Baylor football, horizontal and vertical power estimates may add value when evaluating players whose roles require explosive displacement from static or constrained positions. Staff should also prioritize position-specific agility and skill tests, because the traditional 20-yard shuttle showed limited predictive value outside linebacker sacks.
athleteDev: Development goals should be tied to the football actions that matter for a position. Running backs benefit from speed and explosive horizontal displacement, defensive ends from power out of stance and contact-ready acceleration, and mobile quarterbacks from power qualities that translate to rushing. This supports individualized physical preparation rather than generic combine chasing.
rtp: This is not an RTP study, but the findings can shape late-stage performance testing. Returning athletes should demonstrate restoration of the position-relevant qualities linked to production, such as RB sprint/broad-jump capacity, DE horizontal and vertical power, or QB lower-body power for designed or reactive rushing demands.

citation: Wang H, Martone M, Owens ME, Lemoine NP Jr, Marucci J, Calvert D, Mullenix S, Church TS, Rood J, Harrell B, Irving BA, Spielmann G, Johannsen NM. NCAA Division I American football players with sickle cell trait have altered hematological responses and hydration status. Scientific Reports. 2021;11:1844. doi:10.1038/s41598-021-81473-4
abstract: This longitudinal field study examined hematological responses and hydration status in NCAA Division I American football players with sickle cell trait (SCT). Across two years, 10 SCT players and 10 position-matched controls from a southeastern U.S. university were assessed at pre-camp, post-camp/preseason, and post-season. Blood testing included hemoglobin electrophoresis, complete blood counts, and chemistry panels; hydration assessment during preseason camp included body-weight change, urine specific gravity, urine electrolytes, and sweat electrolytes. SCT players had confirmed HbS values averaging 37.9%, higher red cell distribution width, higher serum uric acid, and higher pre-camp creatine kinase than controls. During camp, SCT players showed lower urine specific gravity and urine electrolytes but higher sweat sodium. The authors conclude that altered kidney and muscle-damage markers may help inform SCT surveillance and that hydration education remains critical.
tldr: SCT players in Division I football showed measurable hematological and hydration differences across a season, including higher RDW, uric acid, and pre-camp CK, plus different urine and sweat electrolyte patterns. For Baylor staff, SCT status should trigger deliberate heat, hydration, acclimatization, and biomarker surveillance rather than a generic participation restriction.
methods: Longitudinal observational study over two football seasons. Participants were 10 NCAA Division I football players with SCT and 10 position-matched controls with similar anthropometrics and football exposure. Blood was collected pre-camp, about 48 hours post-camp/preseason, and about 48 hours after the final competition. Measures included hemoglobin variants, complete blood counts, chemistry panel, estimated glomerular filtration rate, body-mass change, urine specific gravity, urine electrolytes, sweat electrolytes, and wet-bulb globe temperature during camp.
findings: SCT players averaged 37.9% HbS and had greater red cell distribution width than controls across visits. Serum uric acid was higher in SCT players (7.3 +/- 1.0 mg/dL vs 6.1 +/- 0.6 mg/dL; p=0.001). Pre-camp creatine kinase was higher in SCT players (1617.0 +/- 1034.8 IU/L vs 1037.4 +/- 602.8 IU/L; p=0.03). SCT players had lower pre- and post-practice urine specific gravity and urine electrolytes during camp, but higher sweat sodium concentrations (55.4 +/- 13.6 mmol/L vs 45.5 +/- 10.6 mmol/L; p<0.001).
limitations: Small sample size from one Division I program limits generalizability. Three SCT players were enrolled in both years, which may reduce independence and obscure true variability. Fluid intake during camp practices was not measured, so the cause of lower urine electrolytes and urine specific gravity cannot be determined. The field-based observational design improves applied relevance but limits control over confounding variables and exact assessment timing.
practicalImplications: SCT athletes should receive repeated education on hydration, heat stress, symptom reporting, and pacing during high-intensity conditioning. Baylor staff can consider preseason and in-season biomarker surveillance for SCT athletes, especially uric acid, CK, renal markers, hydration status, and sweat/urine electrolyte patterns. Slow acclimatization, rest breaks, and clear emergency action plans remain essential.
athleteDev: For athlete development, SCT should be integrated into individualized conditioning plans. Progressions should avoid abrupt high-volume or high-intensity spikes, especially in heat and humidity, and should include gradual acclimatization. Education should be delivered repeatedly to athletes, coaches, strength staff, and athletic trainers so SCT management is operational rather than only a preseason screening item.
rtp: For SCT athletes returning from illness, exertional symptoms, heat illness, or high-load layoffs, RTP should be conservative and staged. Clearance should include hydration status, symptom resolution, renal and muscle-damage markers when indicated, heat-acclimatization status, and tolerance of progressively intense football conditioning with adequate rest intervals.

citation: Gardner CH, Kotlier JL, Fathi A, Castonguay J, Thompson AA, Bolia IK, Petrigliano FA, Liu JN, Weber AE, Gamradt SC. NCAA football players are at higher risk of upper extremity injury after first-time concussion. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. Published online March 5, 2024. doi:10.1080/00913847.2024.2327275
abstract: This retrospective cohort study tested whether NCAA Division I football players are at increased risk of upper-extremity musculoskeletal injury after a first diagnosed concussion. De-identified injury data from a single Division I conference database were queried for football players diagnosed with their first concussion from 2017-2021. After exclusions, 160 players were reviewed for upper-extremity injuries in the year before concussion and in the year beginning 90 days after concussion. The odds of upper-extremity injury were 2.36 times higher after first concussion than before it (95% CI 1.13-4.95, p=0.02). Shoulder injuries accounted for 57.7% of post-concussion upper-extremity injuries, followed by hand, elbow, and forearm injuries. The authors suggest concussion rehabilitation should account for longer-term musculoskeletal injury risk beyond symptom resolution.
tldr: After a first diagnosed concussion, NCAA football players had more than double the odds of a later upper-extremity injury, with the shoulder most commonly involved. For Baylor football, concussion RTP should not stop at symptom and cognitive clearance; it should include upper-extremity coordination, contact readiness, shoulder control, and football-specific dual-task work.
methods: Retrospective review of the Pac-12 Sports Injury Research Archive for NCAA Division I football players with first diagnosed concussion from 2017-2021. Each included athlete served as his own pre/post comparison. Upper-extremity injuries were counted during the year before concussion and during the year beginning 90 days after concussion. Odds ratios, 95% confidence intervals, and p values were calculated for pre- versus post-concussion injury risk.
findings: A total of 160 football players met inclusion criteria. Upper-extremity injury odds were 2.36 times higher in the year after first diagnosed concussion than in the year before concussion (95% CI 1.13-4.95, p=0.02). Shoulder was the most common injury site after concussion (57.7%), followed by hand (19.2%), elbow (15.4%), forearm (7.7%), and wrist (0%). The finding extends the known post-concussion musculoskeletal injury concern beyond lower-extremity injuries.
limitations: The database did not include concussion history before NCAA participation, playing time, position, age, starter status, or enough demographic detail to build reliable non-concussed matched controls. The authors could not determine specific pathology or mechanisms for upper-extremity injuries. Career progression may naturally increase injury exposure, so the design may overestimate the concussion-specific contribution to later upper-extremity injury.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine and performance staff should monitor shoulder and upper-extremity injury risk after concussion clearance. Baylor concussion RTP protocols should include upper-extremity neuromuscular control, postural control, catching/blocking/contact progressions, and cognitive-motor dual-task challenges. A player who is symptom-free may still need targeted work to reduce subsequent musculoskeletal injury risk.
athleteDev: Athletes with concussion history may need additional development around visual-motor integration, bracing mechanics, tackling/blocking posture, and shoulder girdle control. Incorporating these elements into routine post-concussion return progressions could reduce downstream shoulder and arm injuries during a season.
rtp: RTP after concussion should include progressive exertion plus football-specific upper-extremity demands, not just symptom resolution and neurocognitive testing. Late-stage progression should include catching, ball security, blocking, tackling technique, reactive bracing, and dual-task drills before full contact exposure.

citation: Cole WR, Brett BL, Lempke LB, Mannix R, DeFreese JD, Echemendia RJ, McCrea M, Guskiewicz KM, Meehan WP III. Neither American Football Playing Position Nor Total Years Playing Football Are Associated with Neurobehavioral Health Outcomes: An NFL-LONG Study. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2025;24(11):372-379.
abstract: This cross-sectional NFL-LONG study examined whether primary football position or total years playing football were associated with concussion history and neurobehavioral health outcomes in former NFL players. The final sample included 1,544 former NFL players who completed online or paper questionnaires. Outcomes were PROMIS Cognitive, Depression, and Anxiety T-scores and Neuro-QOL Emotional-Behavioral Dyscontrol T-scores. Positions were grouped as offensive/defensive linemen, running back/linebacker/tight end, quarterback/wide receiver/defensive back, and special teams. Years played did not differ meaningfully across position groups, and after adjusting for concussion history and health covariates, position group, total years played, and their interaction were not statistically or clinically associated with worse neurobehavioral outcomes. Linemen and RB/LB/TE groups reported higher rates of 10 or more concussions, but position and years played were poor standalone proxies for long-term neurobehavioral status.
tldr: In a large sample of former NFL players, position group and total years playing football did not meaningfully predict cognitive, depression, anxiety, or emotional-behavioral dyscontrol scores once concussion history and health factors were considered. For Baylor staff, this argues against using position or career length alone as a head-impact risk story; direct concussion history, symptoms, sleep, pain, and mental health screening matter more.
methods: Cross-sectional analysis within the NFL-LONG study. Recruitment occurred from January 1, 2019 to June 1, 2023 through retired player networks and NFL-related partners. From 1,983 former NFL players with at least one NFL season, 1,544 had complete primary-position data and were included. Questionnaires captured demographics, football history, concussion history, health covariates, and PROMIS/Neuro-QOL outcomes. Analysis of variance tested years played across position groups, and multivariable regressions tested associations among position, years played, their interaction, and neurobehavioral outcomes while adjusting for covariates.
findings: Participants averaged 50.27 +/- 16.4 years old and 17.68 +/- 4.32 total years playing football. Position distribution was 36.2% OL/DL, 30.5% RB/LB/TE, 28.6% QB/WR/DB, and 4.7% special teams. A history of at least one concussion was reported by 72.9%, and 12.6% reported 10 or more concussions. OL/DL and RB/LB/TE groups had higher rates of 10 or more concussions, but position group, years played, and their interaction were not meaningfully associated with PROMIS cognitive, depression, anxiety, or Neuro-QOL emotional-behavioral dyscontrol outcomes after adjustment.
limitations: The study relied on self-reported concussion history, football exposure, and symptoms, creating recall and attribution bias. Concussions from earlier playing eras may have been underrecognized or inconsistently documented. The cross-sectional design cannot determine causality or symptom trajectories. Position and total years played are broad exposure proxies and do not capture actual head-impact counts, severity, practice exposure, style of play, or individual medical history.
practicalImplications: Do not infer long-term neurobehavioral risk from position group or years played alone. Baylor staff should prioritize direct concussion assessment, symptom tracking, sleep screening, pain interference, mental health monitoring, and individualized clinical history. Exposure proxies can support context, but they should not replace direct measures in player health decisions.
athleteDev: Development systems should teach athletes and coaches that concussion reporting and symptom care are more informative than assumptions based on position. Even if certain roles have higher reported concussion counts, individual monitoring should drive education, risk management, and referral decisions.
rtp: The study does not provide acute RTP criteria, but it supports careful concussion history collection and ongoing neurobehavioral monitoring after athletes return. Position and years played should not be used as shortcuts for RTP risk; individualized symptoms, objective testing, sleep, pain, mental health, and clinical exam should guide decisions.
